title: Becoming a Word Detective: Understanding Prefixes
explanation_chunks: This page helps us become super text detectives! We learn to look closely at different types of writing like timelines, biographies, and autobiographies to spot their special features. Some texts focus on 'facts', while others include 'opinions' or have a 'formal' (serious) or 'informal' (chatty) tone.

We also get to discover the power of 'prefixes'. A prefix is a small group of letters that we add to the very *beginning* of a word. When you add a prefix, it changes the meaning of the original word.
For example, the prefix 're-' means 'again'. So, if you 're-read' a book, you read it again! Or if you 'co-' (meaning 'join or with') 'operate' with someone, you work together.
Knowing prefixes is like having a secret code to unlock the meaning of many new words you might come across. It helps you understand what a word means even if you've never seen it before!
examples: The prefix 'un-' means 'not', so 'unhappy' means 'not happy'.
The prefix 'anti-' means 'against' or 'opposite', so 'anti-social' means 'against being social'.
The prefix 'tele-' means 'at a distance', so a 'telephone' lets you hear voices from a distance.
key_points: Different types of writing (timelines, biographies, autobiographies) have specific features like facts, opinions, and tone.
A prefix is a group of letters added to the start of a word to change its meaning.
Learning common prefixes helps you understand new words and expand your vocabulary.
Creating a personal timeline helps you practice organizing events in chronological order.

vocabulary.definition: a group of letters added to the beginning of a word to change its meaning.
the basic part of a word to which prefixes or suffixes are added.
a serious, official way of writing or speaking.
a relaxed, friendly way of writing or speaking.
a diagram showing events in chronological order.

explain_again: This page helps us find clues in stories to see if they're about facts or feelings, and how serious they sound. The really cool part is learning about 'prefixes'! These are little bits you stick at the *start* of a word to change its meaning, like adding 'un-' to 'tie' makes it 'untie'. It's like having a secret power to understand words!
fun_analogy: Prefixes are like secret codes at the beginning of words that change their whole mission! If 'happy' is a superhero, adding 'un-' (the 'not' prefix) turns him into 'unhappy', a less happy version. A timeline is like your life's greatest hits album, but in order.
